subject: Chemistry
q: The osmotic pressure of a solution can be raised by
question: The osmotic pressure of a solution can be raised by
solution:
The correct option is C: increasing the moles of solute.

For a dilute solution, osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = CRT $$

where $\pi$ is osmotic pressure, $C$ is molar concentration of the solution, $R$ is gas constant, and $T$ is temperature.

Also,

$$ C = \frac{n}{V} $$

So,

$$ \pi = \frac{n}{V}RT $$

This shows that osmotic pressure increases when the concentration of solute increases.

If we increase the moles of solute, the value of $n$ increases, so osmotic pressure also increases.

The other options do not increase osmotic pressure:

  • Decreasing temperature decreases $\pi$.

  • Diluting the solution decreases concentration, so $\pi$ decreases.

  • Increasing the moles of solvent also dilutes the solution, so $\pi$ decreases.

So, the answer is Option C: increasing the moles of solute.

subject: Chemistry
q: At 273 K, 10 g of glucose, 10 g of urea and 10 g of sucrose are separately dissolved in 250 mL of water. If their osmotic pressures are P1, P2 and P3, respectively, then the correct order is :
question: At 273 K, 10 g of glucose, 10 g of urea and 10 g of sucrose are separately dissolved in 250 mL of water. If their osmotic pressures are P1, P2 and P3, respectively, then the correct order is :
solution:
Osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = CRT $$

where $C$ is molarity, $R$ is gas constant and $T$ is temperature.

Here all three solutions are at the same temperature, $273\,K$, and each solute is dissolved in the same volume of water, $250\,mL$.

So osmotic pressure depends only on the number of moles of solute present.

$$ \pi \propto n $$

Now, number of moles:

For glucose, molar mass $= 180\,g\,mol^{-1}$

$$ n_1 = \frac{10}{180} $$

For urea, molar mass $= 60\,g\,mol^{-1}$

$$ n_2 = \frac{10}{60} $$

For sucrose, molar mass $= 342\,g\,mol^{-1}$

$$ n_3 = \frac{10}{342} $$

Since

$$ \frac{10}{60} > \frac{10}{180} > \frac{10}{342} $$

we get

$$ P_2 > P_1 > P_3 $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option C: } P_2 > P_1 > P_3} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: Blood is isotonic to which of the following?
question: Blood is isotonic to which of the following?
solution:
Blood is isotonic with $0.9\% \,(\text{w/v})$ NaCl solution.

So, the correct option is:

Option A: $0.9\% \,(\text{w/v})$ NaCl solution

Here, $0.9\% \,(\text{w/v})$ means:

$$ 0.9 \text{ g of NaCl in } 100 \text{ mL of solution} $$

This concentration has nearly the same osmotic pressure as blood plasma. So, red blood cells neither swell nor shrink in this solution.

The other options are not suitable:

$0.9 \,M$ NaCl is much more concentrated than blood.

$0.09 \,M$ NaCl is less concentrated than normal saline.

$9.0\% \,(\text{w/v})$ NaCl is highly concentrated.

Hence, blood is isotonic with $0.9\% \,(\text{w/v})$ NaCl solution.

subject: Chemistry
q: At 27°C, the osmotic pressure of blood is 7.40 atm. For preparing an intravenous glucose solution with osmotic pressure equal to that of blood, the moles of glucose required in one litre of solution are :
question: At 27°C, the osmotic pressure of blood is 7.40 atm. For preparing an intravenous glucose solution with osmotic pressure equal to that of blood, the moles of glucose required in one litre of solution are :
solution:
For glucose solution, glucose is a non-electrolyte, so it does not dissociate in water.

Osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = CRT $$

where

$\pi = 7.40 \text{ atm}$

$T = 27^\circ \text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$

$R = 0.0821 \text{ L atm mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

$C =$ molarity of glucose solution

So,

$$ C = \frac{\pi}{RT} $$

$$ C = \frac{7.40}{0.0821 \times 300} $$

$$ C = \frac{7.40}{24.63} $$

$$ C \approx 0.30 \text{ mol L}^{-1} $$

So, the number of moles of glucose required per litre is

$$ 0.3 \text{ mol} $$

Correct option: A, $0.3$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following is most suitable for use as a semipermeable membrane?
question: Which one of the following is most suitable for use as a semipermeable membrane?
solution:
The best semipermeable membrane among the given options is:

$$\boxed{\text{Copper ferrocyanide}}$$

Copper ferrocyanide is commonly used as an artificial semipermeable membrane. It allows solvent molecules, like water, to pass through but does not allow solute particles to pass through.

Vegetable membrane, animal membrane, and cellophane can also show semipermeable nature, but copper ferrocyanide serves best for this purpose, especially in experiments related to osmosis and osmotic pressure.

So, the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option A: Copper ferrocyanide}}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: In red blood cells, the sum of concentrations of all dissolved particles is nearly 0.30 M, and the cell membrane allows only certain particles to pass through it. If these cells are taken out of blood plasma and kept in pure water at 298 K, what will be the osmotic pressure (in atmosphere) inside the cells?
question: In red blood cells, the sum of concentrations of all dissolved particles is nearly 0.30 M, and the cell membrane allows only certain particles to pass through it. If these cells are taken out of blood plasma and kept in pure water at 298 K, what will be the osmotic pressure (in atmosphere) inside the cells?
solution:
The osmotic pressure is given by the NCERT formula:

$$ \pi = C R T $$

Here, the total concentration of dissolved particles inside the red blood cells is

$$ C = 0.30 \, \text{M} $$

Temperature is

$$ T = 298 \, \text{K} $$

Gas constant is

$$ R = 0.0821 \, \text{L atm mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1} $$

So,

$$ \pi = 0.30 \times 0.0821 \times 298 $$

$$ \pi \approx 7.34 \, \text{atm} $$

Since the cells are placed in pure water, the outside concentration is nearly zero. So water will enter the cells due to osmosis, and the osmotic pressure difference is about $7.34 \, \text{atm}$.

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{7.34 \, \text{atm}} $$

Option A.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Study the following cases :

I : 2M CH3COOH solution in benzene at 27°C in which complete dimer formation takes place

II : 0.5 M KCl aq. solution at 27°C, which undergoes 100% ionisation

Which statement(s) is/are correct :
question:
Study the following cases :

I : 2M CH3COOH solution in benzene at 27°C in which complete dimer formation takes place

II : 0.5 M KCl aq. solution at 27°C, which undergoes 100% ionisation

Which statement(s) is/are correct :
solution:
For osmotic pressure, we use

$$ \pi = iCRT $$

where $i$ is the van’t Hoff factor.

For two solutions at the same temperature, if $iC$ is the same, then their osmotic pressures are the same. Such solutions are isotonic.

For case I:

Acetic acid dimerises in benzene:

$$ 2CH_3COOH \rightarrow (CH_3COOH)_2 $$

Dimer formation is $100\%$, so the number of solute particles becomes half.

So,

$$ i = \frac{1}{2} $$

Given concentration:

$$ C = 2M $$

Hence,

$$ iC = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 = 1 $$

For case II:

KCl ionises completely in water:

$$ KCl \rightarrow K^+ + Cl^- $$

So one formula unit gives two ions.

Thus,

$$ i = 2 $$

Given concentration:

$$ C = 0.5M $$

Hence,

$$ iC = 2 \times 0.5 = 1 $$

Both solutions have the same value of $iC$ and are at the same temperature, so their osmotic pressures are equal.

Thus, both are isotonic.

Correct option: A.

subject: Chemistry
q: At 300 K, equal volumes of a M/20 glucose solution and a M/20 sucrose solution are combined, with the temperature remaining unchanged. If the osmotic pressures of the glucose solution, the sucrose solution and the resulting mixture are π1, π2 and π3, respectively, then
question: At 300 K, equal volumes of a M/20 glucose solution and a M/20 sucrose solution are combined, with the temperature remaining unchanged. If the osmotic pressures of the glucose solution, the sucrose solution and the resulting mixture are π1, π2 and π3, respectively, then
solution:
For dilute solutions, osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = C R T $$

where $C$ is the molar concentration of solute particles.

Glucose and sucrose are both non-electrolytes, so they do not dissociate in solution. Hence, for both solutions, the van’t Hoff factor is $1$.

Given:

$$ C_{\text{glucose}} = \frac{M}{20} $$

and

$$ C_{\text{sucrose}} = \frac{M}{20} $$

Both are at the same temperature, $300 \, K$.

So,

$$ \pi_1 = \frac{1}{20}RT $$

and

$$ \pi_2 = \frac{1}{20}RT $$

Thus,

$$ \pi_1 = \pi_2 $$

Now equal volumes of the two solutions are mixed.

Suppose volume of each solution is $V$.

Moles of glucose:

$$ \frac{1}{20} \times V $$

Moles of sucrose:

$$ \frac{1}{20} \times V $$

Total moles of solute particles:

$$ \frac{V}{20} + \frac{V}{20} = \frac{2V}{20} $$

Total volume after mixing:

$$ 2V $$

So concentration of solute particles in the mixture is

$$ \frac{\frac{2V}{20}}{2V} = \frac{1}{20} $$

Hence,

$$ \pi_3 = \frac{1}{20}RT $$

So,

$$ \pi_1 = \pi_2 = \pi_3 $$

Correct option: A

subject: Chemistry
q: Using the principle of reverse osmosis, estimate the minimum pressure needed to remove salt from sea water having 2.5% (w/v) NaCl at 27°C.
question: Using the principle of reverse osmosis, estimate the minimum pressure needed to remove salt from sea water having 2.5% (w/v) NaCl at 27°C.
solution:
For reverse osmosis, the applied pressure must be at least equal to the osmotic pressure of the seawater solution.

For dilute solutions, osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = iCRT $$

For NaCl, it dissociates as

$$ \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + \text{Cl}^- $$

So, the van’t Hoff factor is approximately

$$ i = 2 $$

Given concentration is $2.5\%$ w/v NaCl.

This means:

$$ 2.5 \text{ g NaCl in } 100 \text{ mL solution} $$

So in $1 \text{ L}$ solution:

$$ 25 \text{ g NaCl} $$

Molar mass of NaCl:

$$ 58.5 \text{ g mol}^{-1} $$

Molarity:

$$ C = \frac{25}{58.5} $$

$$ C \approx 0.427 \text{ mol L}^{-1} $$

Temperature:

$$ T = 27^\circ \text{C} = 300 \text{ K} $$

Now,

$$ \pi = iCRT $$

$$ \pi = 2 \times 0.427 \times 0.0821 \times 300 $$

$$ \pi \approx 21 \text{ atm} $$

So, the approximate minimum pressure required is

$$ \boxed{21 \text{ atm}} $$

Correct option: B.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Statement I : Except osmotic pressure, all other colligative properties depend on the nature of solvent.

Statement II : Colligative properties are intensive properties.
question:
Statement I : Except osmotic pressure, all other colligative properties depend on the nature of solvent.

Statement II : Colligative properties are intensive properties.
options: Both statements are CORRECT, and Statement II is the CORRECT explanation of Statement I.
Both statements are CORRECT, and Statement II is NOT the CORRECT explanation of Statement I.
Statement I is CORRECT, but Statement II is INCORRECT.
Statement I is INCORRECT, but Statement II is CORRECT.
solution:
In NCERT, the vapour pressure colligative property is taken as relative lowering of vapour pressure.

For a dilute ideal solution,

$$ \frac{p_1^0-p_1}{p_1^0}=x_2 $$

Here $x_2$ is the mole fraction of solute.

So relative lowering of vapour pressure depends only on the number of solute particles, not on the nature of the solvent.

For boiling point elevation and freezing point depression,

$$ \Delta T_b=K_b m $$

$$ \Delta T_f=K_f m $$

Here $K_b$ and $K_f$ are constants of the solvent, so these two depend on the nature of the solvent.

For osmotic pressure,

$$ \pi = CRT $$

It depends on concentration and temperature, not on the nature of the solvent.

So Statement I is incorrect because relative lowering of vapour pressure also does not depend on the nature of the solvent.

Statement II is correct because colligative properties depend on concentration, not on the total amount of solution. So they are intensive properties.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution made by dissolving 4.0 g of PVC in 1 L of dioxane shows an osmotic pressure of 0.006 atm at 300 K. The molecular mass of the polymer PVC is
question: A solution made by dissolving 4.0 g of PVC in 1 L of dioxane shows an osmotic pressure of 0.006 atm at 300 K. The molecular mass of the polymer PVC is
solution:
For osmotic pressure, we use the relation

$$ \pi = \frac{wRT}{MV} $$

where

$\pi = 0.006 \text{ atm}$

$w = 4.0 \text{ g}$

$R = 0.0821 \text{ L atm K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$

$T = 300 \text{ K}$

$V = 1 \text{ L}$

$M =$ molecular mass of PVC

Rearranging the formula,

$$ M = \frac{wRT}{\pi V} $$

Substituting the values,

$$ M = \frac{4.0 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{0.006 \times 1} $$

$$ M = \frac{98.52}{0.006} $$

$$ M = 16420 $$

So, the molecular mass of PVC is

$$ \boxed{16420} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: A $5\%$ solution of a solute (X) has the same osmotic pressure as a $0.855\%$ solution of sucrose (molecular weight $=342$). What is the molecular mass of solute (X)?
question: A $5\%$ solution of a solute (X) has the same osmotic pressure as a $0.855\%$ solution of sucrose (molecular weight $=342$). What is the molecular mass of solute (X)?
solution:
For isotonic solutions, osmotic pressure is the same.

At the same temperature,

$$ \pi = CRT $$

So if two solutions are isotonic, their molar concentrations are equal.

A $5\%$ solution of solute $X$ means:

$$ 5 \text{ g of } X \text{ in } 100 \text{ mL solution} $$

A $0.855\%$ solution of sucrose means:

$$ 0.855 \text{ g of sucrose in } 100 \text{ mL solution} $$

Since both have the same volume, their number of moles must be equal.

Moles of solute $X$:

$$ \frac{5}{M_X} $$

Moles of sucrose:

$$ \frac{0.855}{342} $$

So,

$$ \frac{5}{M_X}=\frac{0.855}{342} $$

Now solve for $M_X$:

$$ M_X=\frac{5 \times 342}{0.855} $$

$$ M_X=\frac{1710}{0.855} $$

$$ M_X=2000 $$

So, the molecular mass of solute $X$ is

$$ \boxed{2000 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q: The osmotic pressure of two aqueous urea solutions-I and II are 2.1 atm and 1.8 atm respectively at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The osmotic pressure (in atm) of a solution at $25^{\circ}$ prepared by mixing solution I and II in the volume ratio of $2: 1$ is
question: The osmotic pressure of two aqueous urea solutions-I and II are 2.1 atm and 1.8 atm respectively at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The osmotic pressure (in atm) of a solution at $25^{\circ}$ prepared by mixing solution I and II in the volume ratio of $2: 1$ is
solution:
For urea solution, osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = CRT $$

Since both solutions are at the same temperature, osmotic pressure is directly proportional to concentration.

So, when two solutions are mixed, the osmotic pressure of the mixture is the volume-weighted average:

$$ \pi_{\text{mix}}=\frac{\pi_1V_1+\pi_2V_2}{V_1+V_2} $$

Given:

$$ \pi_1=2.1 \text{ atm} $$

$$ \pi_2=1.8 \text{ atm} $$

The volume ratio is

$$ V_1:V_2=2:1 $$

So take

$$ V_1=2,\quad V_2=1 $$

Now,

$$ \pi_{\text{mix}}=\frac{(2.1)(2)+(1.8)(1)}{2+1} $$

$$ \pi_{\text{mix}}=\frac{4.2+1.8}{3} $$

$$ \pi_{\text{mix}}=\frac{6.0}{3}=2.0 $$

So, the osmotic pressure of the mixed solution is

$$ \boxed{2.0 \text{ atm}} $$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The freezing point of an aqueous urea solution is found to be $-0.515^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the osmotic pressure (in atm ) of this solution at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.( In Nearest Integer)

(Given : $K_{f\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question:
The freezing point of an aqueous urea solution is found to be $-0.515^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the osmotic pressure (in atm ) of this solution at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.( In Nearest Integer)

(Given : $K_{f\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)}=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
For urea solution, urea is a non-electrolyte, so its van’t Hoff factor is

$$i=1$$

The freezing point depression is

$$\Delta T_f = 0 - (-0.515)=0.515 \text{ K}$$

Using

$$\Delta T_f = K_f m$$

we get

$$0.515 = 1.86 \times m$$

So,

$$m=\frac{0.515}{1.86}$$

$$m \approx 0.277 \text{ mol kg}^{-1}$$

For a dilute aqueous solution, molarity is taken approximately equal to molality:

$$M \approx 0.277 \text{ mol L}^{-1}$$

Osmotic pressure is given by

$$\pi = MRT$$

Here,

$$R=0.0821 \text{ L atm mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$$

and

$$T=37+273=310 \text{ K}$$

So,

$$\pi = 0.277 \times 0.0821 \times 310$$

$$\pi \approx 7.04 \text{ atm}$$

Nearest integer:

$$\boxed{7 \text{ atm}}$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The osmotic pressure of a urea solution at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 500 mm. After dilution of the solution, its temperature is increased to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the osmotic pressure becomes 105.3 mm. Find the extent of dilution.
question: The osmotic pressure of a urea solution at $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is 500 mm. After dilution of the solution, its temperature is increased to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the osmotic pressure becomes 105.3 mm. Find the extent of dilution.
solution:
For a dilute solution, osmotic pressure is given by

$$ \pi = CRT $$

For the same amount of solute, concentration $C$ is inversely proportional to volume $V$.

So,

$$ \pi = \frac{n}{V}RT $$

This gives

$$ \frac{\pi V}{T} = nR $$

Since the amount of urea remains the same before and after dilution,

$$ \frac{\pi_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{\pi_2 V_2}{T_2} $$

Given:

$$ \pi_1 = 500 \text{ mm} $$

$$ T_1 = 10^\circ \text{C} = 283 \text{ K} $$

$$ \pi_2 = 105.3 \text{ mm} $$

$$ T_2 = 25^\circ \text{C} = 298 \text{ K} $$

So,

$$ \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{\pi_1 T_2}{\pi_2 T_1} $$

Substituting the values,

$$ \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{500 \times 298}{105.3 \times 283} $$

$$ \frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{149000}{29799.9} $$

$$ \frac{V_2}{V_1} \approx 5 $$

So, the final volume is $5$ times the original volume.

Hence, the solution has been diluted to $5$ times its original volume, or its volume has been increased by $4$ times the original volume.
